perturbation.original_text: Each Recipient Party with respect to Confidential Information received by it hereunder shall: A. Hold such Confidential Information in strict confidence and protect it in accordance with a standard of care which shall be no less than the care it uses to protect its own information of like importance and in no event less than reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Recipient Party with respect to Confidential Information received by it hereunder shall: A. Endeavor to hold such Confidential Information in confidence and protect it with a level of care they feel is adequate.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states a specific standard of care, whereas the modified text makes it ambiguous by using 'endeavor to hold' and 'level of care they feel is adequate,' which makes the obligation subjective and difficult to enforce.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 812.081 - Theft of trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts Florida Statute 812.081 because it weakens the obligation to protect trade secrets. The statute requires a certain level of protection against misappropriation, which the modified text undermines by allowing the Recipient Party to determine what level of care is adequate, making it easier to claim they did not violate the law.

F.S. 812.081 812.081 Theft of or trafficking in trade secrets; definitions; penalties; providing to foreign entities; restitution. — (1) As used in this section, the term: (a) “Endeavor” means to attempt or to try. (b) “Foreign agent” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government. (c) “Foreign instrumentality” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government. (d) “Obtain or use” has the same meaning as provided in s. 812.012 (3). (e) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (f) “Trade secret” means the whole or any portion or phase of any formula, pattern, device, combination of devices, or compilation of information which is for use, or is used, in the operation of a business and which provides the business an advantage, or an opportunity to obtain an advantage, over those who do not know or use it. The term includes any scientific, technical, or commercial information, including financial information, and includes any design, process, procedure, list of suppliers, list of customers, business code, or improvement thereof, whether tangible or intangible, and regardless of whether or how it is stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing. Irrespective of novelty, invention, patentability, the state of the prior art, and the level of skill in the business, art, or field to which the subject matter pertains, a trade secret is considered to be: 1. Secret; 2. Of value; 3. For use or in use by the business; and 4. Of advantage to the business, or providing an opportunity to obtain an advantage, over
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Florida Statute 812.081. The original contract mandates a specific 'standard of care' to protect confidential information. The changed text replaces this with 'endeavor to hold' and a 'level of care they feel is adequate,' making the obligation subjective. Florida Statute 812.081 defines 'endeavor' as 'to attempt or to try,' which weakens the original obligation. The original text provided a clearer standard which is now undermined, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: C. Not disclose Confidential Information to any person other than to one of the Recipient Party's directors, officers, employees, representatives, consultants or agents (collectively called "Representatives") who need to know such information for the purposes of the Evaluation, provided that each Representative shall be informed, directed and obligated by the Recipient Party to treat such information in accordance with the obligations of this agreement and the Recipient Party shall be liable for breach of any such obligation by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: C. Refrain from disclosing Confidential Information to any person other than to one of the Recipient Party's directors, officers, employees, representatives, consultants or agents (collectively called "Representatives") who may potentially benefit from knowing such information for the purposes of the Evaluation, and the Recipient Party will make a good faith effort to ensure such representatives uphold the agreement's confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly limits disclosure to those who 'need to know' and holds the Recipient Party liable for breaches by Representatives. The modified text broadens the scope to those who 'may potentially benefit' and weakens the liability by requiring only a 'good faith effort'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 688.003 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts Florida's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (FUTSA), specifically F.S. 688.003, which defines misappropriation of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to prevent disclosure and the liability for representatives' breaches, the modified text makes it more difficult to prove misappropriation occurred, as the 'good faith effort' standard is much lower than a strict liability standard for representatives' actions.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 003 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 003 Injunctive relief. F.S. 688.003 688.003 Injunctive relief. — (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History. — s. 3, ch. 88-254.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the protection of trade secrets, creating a contradiction with Florida's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (FUTSA). The original text required strict adherence to confidentiality obligations and held the Recipient Party liable for breaches by Representatives. The modified text, however, only requires a 'good faith effort' to ensure confidentiality, which is a significantly lower standard than strict liability. This makes it more difficult to prove misappropriation under F.S. 688.003, which allows for injunctive relief for 'actual or threatened misappropriation'. The 'good faith effort' standard is not sufficient to ensure that misappropriation does not occur.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Each Recipient Party shall notify the Disclosing Party in the event that any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party is required to be disclosed by the Recipient Party by judicial, administrative or other govern mental order. Such notification shall be made reasonably promptly so that the Disclosing Party, at its expense, may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy. Provided such notification is given, the Recipient Party is hereby authorized to comply with such judicial, administrative or governmental order.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. The Recipient Party will endeavor to notify the Disclosing Party if Confidential Information is requested to be disclosed by judicial, administrative, or other governmental order, giving the Disclosing Party the option to seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy. In the event notification is made, the Recipient Party is authorized to comply with such order.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall notify' to 'will endeavor to notify' creates uncertainty about whether the notification will actually occur, and 'requested' instead of 'required' implies a lower level of obligation to disclose the information. Removing 'reasonably promptly' also introduces ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 1.280(c)
perturbation.law_citation: Florida Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 1.280(c)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts Florida Rules of Civil Procedure, specifically Rule 1.280(c) regarding protective orders. Rule 1.280(c) allows a party to seek a protective order to protect confidential information during discovery. By weakening the Recipient Party's obligation to notify the Disclosing Party promptly of a subpoena or order requiring disclosure, the modified text hinders the Disclosing Party's ability to seek a protective order in a timely manner, potentially resulting in the disclosure of confidential information that could have been protected.
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to notify, from 'shall notify' to 'will endeavor to notify,' and introduces ambiguity by changing 'required to be disclosed' to 'requested to be disclosed' and removing 'reasonably promptly'. This undermines the Disclosing Party's ability to seek a protective order. Although no scraped law snippets were provided, the perturbation explanation and the law explanation both strongly suggest that these changes create a legal contradiction, because weakening the notification requirement may result in the disclosure of confidential information that could have been protected by a protective order if the original contract language was maintained.

perturbation.original_text: 10. If either Party breaches its promises made in this Agreement, the non-breaching Party will have available to it all remedies available under law.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. If either Party arguably breaches its promises made in this Agreement, the non-breaching Party may explore remedies available under applicable law.
perturbation.explanation: The original text grants the non-breaching party all available legal remedies in the event of a breach. The modified text uses 'arguably breaches' and 'may explore', making the availability of remedies uncertain and subject to interpretation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 672.703 - Seller's Remedies in General for Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: While this agreement isn't solely about the sale of goods, the principle of remedies for breach of contract is universal. Florida Statutes § 672.703 outlines the remedies available to a seller when a buyer breaches a contract for the sale of goods. The modified text contradicts the spirit of contract law and potentially this specific statute by weakening the non-breaching party's right to remedies. By introducing 'arguably breaches' and 'may explore,' the modified text creates uncertainty about whether a breach actually occurred and whether the non-breaching party is entitled to the remedies provided by law, thus undermining the enforceability of the contract.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 672 > Section 703 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 672 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE: SALES Entire Chapter SECTION 703 Seller’s remedies in general. F.S. 672.703 672.703 Seller’s remedies in general. — Where the buyer wrongfully rejects or revokes acceptance of goods or fails to make a payment due on or before delivery or repudiates with respect to a part or the whole, then with respect to any goods directly affected and, if the breach is of the whole contract (s. 672.612 ), then also with respect to the whole undelivered balance, the aggrieved seller may: (1) Withhold delivery of such goods; (2) Stop delivery by any bailee as hereafter provided (s. 672.705 ); (3) Proceed under the next section respecting goods still unidentified to the contract; (4) Resell and recover damages as hereafter provided (s. 672.706 ); (5) Recover damages for nonacceptance (s. 672.708 ) or in a proper case the price (s. 672.709 ); (6) Cancel. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254. Note. — s. 2-703, U.C.C.
perturbation.justification: The original contract provides 'all remedies available under law' in case of a breach. The changed text introduces 'arguably breaches' and 'may explore', weakening the non-breaching party's right to remedies. The Law Explanation argues that this contradicts the spirit of contract law by creating uncertainty about whether a breach occurred and whether remedies are available. While the scraped snippet focuses on seller's remedies under Florida Statutes § 672.703, it supports the general principle that remedies should be available when a breach occurs. The modification introduces ambiguity that undermines the enforceability of the remedies, potentially requiring legal interpretation to determine if the breach is 'arguable' and if the non-breaching party 'may' explore remedies. This directly contradicts the original text and creates an ambiguity that weakens the availability of legal remedies, thus contradicting the law by making enforcement more difficult and uncertain.
